proof_sketch: The mutation changes the guard from idx < total_elements to idx < total_elements-1, skipping the last element (index 524287). For the fixed shape [128,4096] (524288 elements), the last thread is active in the original but inactive in the mutant. The output tensor element_losses is allocated with torch::empty, leaving the last element uninitialized. The reduction kernel then reads this uninitialized value. Under the test environment, memory reuse causes the uninitialized location to retain the value computed by the original kernel from the previous execution of the same test case, making the final mean loss identical. This is a ghost-write equivalence that masks the mutation. A properly isolated execution (or different execution order) would expose the difference.

The last element remains whatever was in memory (since torch::empty, uninitialized). Then the reduction sums all elements, including that uninitialized value. So the final mean loss will differ if the last element's uninitialized memory differs from what the original would have computed.
